root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

electrodynamics I: the basic theory

Table of contents

  1. The electromagnetic field tensor
  2. Maxwell’s laws — coordinate-dependent form
  3. Maxwell’s laws — coordinate-independent form

Prerequisites

The electromagnetic field tensor

Consider a material charged particle moving through an electromagnetic field in euclidean $3$-space with electric and magnetic components $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$. Let $\bp$ denote the proper momentum (or relativistic momentum) of the particle, i.e., the spatial part of the momentum $4$-vector

\begin{equation}\notag p^\mu = (p^0,\bp). \end{equation}

If the particle has charge $q$, then the classical Lorentz force law still holds with $\bp$ in place of the usual momentum:

\begin{equation}\label{lorentz-eqn} \frac{\d \bp}{\d t} = q ( \vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B}). \end{equation}

Here, $t$ stands for the coordinate time and $\vec{v}$ is the ordinary velocity of the particle. The goal in this section is to transport the Lorentz force law to a covariant expression that lives on Minkowski spacetime $\bbm^4$. In particular, we want to produce an expression

\begin{equation}\label{goal-eqn} \frac{\d p^\mu}{\d \tau} = (\text{something}) \end{equation}

that reduces to \eqref{lorentz-eqn} when equating spatial parts. Here, $\tau$ is the proper time of the particle and $p^\mu$ is its momentum $4$-vector defined above.

Now, the spatial part of the left-hand side of \eqref{goal-eqn} is

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\d \bp}{\d \tau} = \gamma \frac{\d \bp}{\d t} = q\left( v^0 \frac{\vec{E}}{c} + \bv \times \vec{B} \right) \end{equation}

where $\bv = \gamma \vec{v}$ is the proper velocity of the particle, which is the spatial part of the velocity $4$-vector

\begin{equation}\notag v^\mu = (\gamma c, \gamma \vec{v}). \end{equation}

The number

\begin{equation}\notag \gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}} \end{equation}

is the usual Lorentz factor (with $v^2 = \vec{v} \cdot \vec{v}$). On the other hand, the temporal component on the left-hand side of \eqref{goal-eqn} is

\begin{equation}\notag \frac{\d p^0}{\d \tau} = \frac{\gamma}{c} \frac{\d E}{\d t} = \frac{\gamma}{c} \frac{\d \bp}{\d t} \cdot \vec{v} = \frac{\gamma}{c} q \vec{E} \cdot \vec{v} = q\frac{\vec{E}}{c} \cdot \bv, \end{equation}

where $E=cp^0$ is the (total) relativistic energy of the particle. If we then define a $\binom{1}{1}$-tensor $F^\mu{}_\nu$ with standard components

\begin{equation}\notag \begin{bmatrix} 0 & E_x/c & E_y/c & E_z/c \\ E_x/c & 0 & B_z & -B_y \\ E_y/c & -B_z & 0 & B_x \\ E_z/c & B_y & -B_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \end{equation}

then an easy check reveals that our desired expression \eqref{goal-eqn} can be given in the covariant form

\begin{equation}\label{lorentz-mink-eqn} \frac{\d p^\mu}{\d \tau} = q F^{\mu}{}_\nu v^\nu. \end{equation}

Using the Minkowski metric $\eta = \eta_{\mu\nu}$ with signature $(+,-,-,-)$, we may lower the first index to obtain the tensor $F_{\mu\nu}$ with components

\begin{equation}\label{matrix-eqn} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & E_x/c & E_y/c & E_z/c \\ -E_x/c & 0 & -B_z & B_y \\ -E_y/c & B_z & 0 & -B_x \\ -E_z/c & -B_y & B_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}. \end{equation}

Then:

Definition. The antisymmetric rank-$2$ covariant tensor $F_{\mu\nu}$ on $\bbm^4$ with standard components \eqref{matrix-eqn} is called the electromagnetic field tensor. The equation \eqref{lorentz-mink-eqn} is called the relativistic Lorentz force law.

Maxwell’s laws — coordinate-dependent form

We categorize Maxwell’s classical equations of electrodynamics into the magnetostatic and magnetodynamic equations

\begin{equation}\label{magnetic-eqn} \nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla \times \vec{E} + \frac{\bd \vec{B}}{\bd t} = 0, \end{equation}

and the electrostatic and electrodynamic equations

\begin{equation}\label{electric-eqn} \nabla \cdot \vec{E} = \rho/\dev_0 \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla \times \vec{B}-\mu_0\dev_0 \frac{\bd \vec{E}}{\bd t} = \mu_0 \vec{J}. \end{equation}

Here, $\rho$ and $\vec{J}$ are the charge and current densities, respectively, while $\dev_0$ and $\mu_0$ are (small) physical constants.

Maxwell’s laws — coordinate-independent form
